channel.title: Hapi — Happy News by Topic
channel.description: Uplifting stories from around the world, organised by topic.

item.title: Africa's Tech Startups Raise Record $382M in Q1 2026, Up 35%
item.description: African technology startups secured $382 million in funding in Q1 2026 — a 35% jump from the same period in 2025 and the continent's strongest opening quarter on record. Egypt led with $190 million, followed by South Africa and Kenya. The milestone signals Africa's startup ecosystem entering a mature phase, with Japanese investors joining traditional US and European backers as confidence in the continent's innovation potential soars.

item.title: Apple Reports Record 30% Recycled Materials — Plastic Fully Eliminated From Packaging
item.description: Apple's 2026 Environmental Progress Report revealed a record 30% of material across all products shipped in 2025 came from recycled content, including 100% recycled cobalt in every battery and rare earth elements in every magnet. The company fully eliminated plastic from all packaging, diverted 600,000+ metric tons of waste from landfills, and cut greenhouse gas emissions by over 60% since 2015 — cementing its place as a corporate sustainability benchmark.

item.title: Paraguay Posts South America's Fastest Economic Growth — 6.6% in 2025
item.description: Paraguay's GDP grew 6.6% in 2025, the fastest expansion in 12 years and the strongest performance in South America for the second consecutive year, driven by a 10.5% surge in agriculture and 10.9% growth in hydroelectric energy. The IMF upgraded the country's 2026 outlook and Moody's awarded Paraguay its first-ever investment-grade credit rating, placing this landlocked nation firmly on the map for global investors.

item.title: Breakthrough Prize Honours Scientists Who Restored Sight to Children Born Blind
item.description: Scientists Jean Bennett, Katherine High, and Albert Maguire received the $3 million Breakthrough Prize in Life Sciences for developing Luxturna — the first FDA-approved gene replacement therapy for inherited retinal disease. Children who previously could only perceive bright light can now attend regular schools and play outdoors at night, marking the first time a single gene correction has permanently restored sight in people born with inherited blindness.

item.title: Moringa 'Miracle Tree' Removes 98% of Microplastics From Drinking Water
item.description: Researchers at São Paulo State University found that seed extract from the moringa tree removes over 98% of PVC microplastics from water, outperforming aluminum sulfate across a wider pH range. The plant-based solution is biodegradable, non-toxic, and carbon-sequestering — a quiet revolution in clean water access for communities worldwide. The study, published in ACS Omega, has drawn global attention for its elegant, nature-derived simplicity.

item.title: Muon g-2 Wins Breakthrough Prize for Most Precise Subatomic Measurement in History
item.description: The Muon g-2 collaboration at Fermilab, CERN, and Brookhaven received the $3 million Breakthrough Prize in Fundamental Physics for measuring the muon's magnetic moment to 127 parts per billion — 30,000 times more precise than the first attempt in 1965. The result offers the most tantalising experimental hint yet that undiscovered particles or forces may govern our universe, pointing toward new physics beyond the Standard Model.

item.title: UK Renewables Top 52% for Second Year — 2025 Was First Year With Zero Coal
item.description: Renewable energy generated a record 52.5% of UK electricity in 2025, the second consecutive year above 50%. Wind contributed 30% of total generation and solar hit a new high of 20 TWh. Most remarkably, 2025 was the first year in over 140 years with zero coal-fired electricity in Britain — a milestone RenewableUK called proof that renewables are now the backbone of the nation's power system.

item.title: India Crosses Historic 150 GW Solar Milestone With Record Annual Additions
item.description: India's cumulative solar capacity surpassed 150 gigawatts after adding a record 44.6 GW in fiscal year 2026 — an 87% year-on-year increase and the fastest any nation has ever bridged a 50 GW solar increment, achieved in just 14 months. PM Modi's Surya Ghar rooftop scheme benefited roughly 2.6 million homes, and India is now the world's third-largest solar market, on track to become the second-largest by end of 2026.

item.title: All Six 2026 Goldman Environmental Prize Winners Are Women — A Historic First
item.description: For the first time in its 37-year history, the Goldman Environmental Prize honoured six women from six continents. Winners stopped a mega mine in Alaska, secured Rio Tinto's first mining accountability in Papua New Guinea, halted fracking in Colombia, and won a landmark UK Supreme Court ruling on fossil fuels. Nigeria's Iroro Tanshi was celebrated for protecting endangered bats and securing 27,000 community livelihoods at Afi Mountain.

item.title: Chile Becomes the First Country in the Americas to Eliminate Leprosy
item.description: The World Health Organization officially verified Chile as leprosy-free in March 2026 — the first nation in the Americas and only the second globally after Jordan to achieve this milestone. Chile has recorded no locally transmitted cases in over 30 years, the result of decades of sustained surveillance, early diagnosis, and political commitment. WHO and PAHO praised the achievement as a global model for disease elimination.

item.title: US Traffic Deaths Drop 6.7% in 2025, Second-Lowest Rate in Over a Century
item.description: The US Department of Transportation announced that an estimated 36,640 people died in traffic crashes in 2025 — a 6.7% drop from 2024 and the second-lowest fatality rate (1.10 per 100 million miles) in over 104 years of recordkeeping. Deaths declined in 39 states, the District of Columbia, and Puerto Rico, reflecting years of investment in road safety technology, infrastructure, and evidence-based enforcement.

item.title: Scientists Map the Entire Nerve Network of the Clitoris for the First Time
item.description: Researchers at Amsterdam University Medical Center used synchrotron X-rays to create the world's first high-resolution 3D map of every nerve pathway in the human clitoris, overturning decades of incomplete anatomical understanding. The breakthrough provides a critical surgical roadmap for gender-affirming procedures and reconstructive surgery for the estimated 230 million survivors of female genital mutilation, marking a landmark advance in women's health science.

item.title: Rwanda Opens Visa-Free Travel to All 55 African Union Member States
item.description: Rwanda has extended full visa-free access to citizens of all 55 African Union member states, allowing 30-day stays without prior authorisation, joined by Ghana, Kenya, Tanzania, Seychelles, Benin, and The Gambia. The policy is already driving measurable growth in cross-border arrivals and hospitality investment. Analysts credit the initiative with accelerating AfCFTA economic integration and positioning East Africa as the continent's most open travel destination.

item.title: US Adult Smoking Rate Falls Below 10% for the First Time in Recorded History
item.description: CDC data confirmed the adult cigarette smoking rate in the United States dropped to 9.9% in 2024 — the first time in recorded history it has fallen to single digits, down from a peak of 42.4% in 1965. The 77% decline over six decades reflects the combined power of taxation, advertising bans, cessation programmes, and generational shifts in social norms — one of modern public health's greatest sustained achievements.

item.title: Zimbabwe's Vaccine Cyclists Pedal Through Elephant Country to Protect 22,000 Girls
item.description: Community health workers in rural Zimbabwe are cycling door-to-door through heat, thorns, and elephant country to deliver HPV vaccines to girls far beyond clinic reach. Backed by UNICEF and the Clinton Health Access Initiative, the programme has immunised over 22,000 girls in Mashonaland West Province — a joyful act of prevention against a disease that kills two-thirds of Zimbabwean women diagnosed with cervical cancer.

item.title: Robert Plant Honoured as Record Store Day Legend at World's Oldest Record Shop
item.description: Led Zeppelin frontman Robert Plant was named the third-ever Record Store Day Legend — after Elton John and Johnny Marr — in a ceremony at Spillers Records in Cardiff, the world's oldest record shop. On April 18, thousands of independent record stores worldwide celebrated Record Store Day's 19th year with exclusive vinyl releases and in-store performances, with Plant declaring that record shops have always been a part of his life.

item.title: San Francisco Art Fair Dedicates Entire 2026 Programme to AAPI Artists
item.description: The 2026 San Francisco Art Fair at Fort Mason dedicated its entire programming to Asian American and Pacific Islander artists, partnering with the Asian Art Museum and Stanford's AAPI Art Initiative. Featuring 90 galleries, participatory performances, and a preview of work headed to the 2026 Venice Biennale, the four-day event celebrated the Bay Area's immigrant legacies and amplified voices that have long shaped but rarely headlined the contemporary art world.

item.title: 92nd Drama League Awards Celebrate a Diverse and Joyful Broadway Season
item.description: The Drama League announced nominees for its 92nd annual awards on April 20, honouring a broad Broadway season featuring Schmigadoon!, Titanique, Ragtime, and Chess. Distinguished Performance nominees include Daniel Radcliffe, Ayo Edebiri, Laurie Metcalf, and Lea Michele. The ceremony, set for May 15 at the Ziegfeld Ballroom, caps what critics are calling one of the most culturally vibrant Broadway years in a decade.

item.title: John Korir Shatters Boston Marathon Course Record With 2:01:52
item.description: Kenyan runner John Korir won his second consecutive Boston Marathon on April 20, obliterating the previous course record of 2:03:02 with a time of 2:01:52 — the fifth-fastest marathon ever run. Three finishers in the same race surpassed the old record, making it the fastest field in the event's 130-year history. Korir and his brother Wesley are now the only sibling duo to have both won the iconic race.

item.title: Sharon Lokedi Defends Boston Marathon Title, Clocks Fourth-Fastest Women's Time Ever
item.description: Kenya's Sharon Lokedi successfully defended her Boston Marathon crown on April 20, winning in 2:18:51 — the fourth-fastest women's finish in the race's 130-year history — despite forgetting her GPS watch at the start. Her dominant back-to-back victory, secured by nearly a minute over the field, cements Lokedi as one of distance running's defining champions and adds another luminous chapter to Kenya's extraordinary marathon legacy.

item.title: New Zealand Sweeps the Oceania Archery Target Championships on Home Soil
item.description: New Zealand archers dominated the 2026 World Archery Oceania Target Championships in Auckland, winning gold in every individual recurve and compound division. Ben McLean and Julia Harrison won the recurve events, while 54-year-old Lisa Walker claimed compound gold. The championships brought together 83 archers from 10 nations, with New Zealand topping the medal table as fans cheered through windy waterfront conditions.

item.title: Europe's Pact for Skills Reaches 10 Million Workers Trained
item.description: Since its 2020 launch, the EU's Pact for Skills has upskilled 10 million workers — 3.9 million in 2025 alone — through 4,000 partner organisations and more than €1 billion in collective investment. With 25 million people targeted by 2030, it is reshaping how Europe builds the green and digital workforce it needs.

item.title: Africa's Solar Sector Installs More in One Quarter Than in All of Last Year
item.description: Africa added nearly 1 GW of utility-scale solar in Q1 2026 — more than any previous full year — led by South Africa and Egypt. The milestone is drawing fresh international investment and accelerating a clean energy boom that is creating jobs and powering communities across the continent.

item.title: South Korea Tourism Hits Record High With 4.76 Million Arrivals in Q1 2026
item.description: International visitors to South Korea surged 23% year-on-year in the first quarter of 2026, reaching a record 4.76 million. Fuelled by the global K-pop wave, new long-haul routes and cultural events across Seoul, Busan and Jeju, the boom is generating billions for local businesses and communities.

item.title: Northwestern Engineers Print Artificial Neurons That Communicate With Real Brain Cells
item.description: Northwestern University researchers built flexible, printable artificial neurons from nanoscale graphene and molybdenum disulfide that successfully triggered responses in living mouse brain tissue. The study, published in Nature Nanotechnology, marks a leap toward neuroprosthetics, brain-machine interfaces and AI that is orders of magnitude more energy-efficient.

item.title: Graphene Electrons Flow Like a Frictionless Liquid, Defying a Core Law of Physics
item.description: IISc scientists and collaborators in Japan observed electrons in graphene behaving as a "Dirac fluid" near a special quantum boundary, deviating from the Wiedemann-Franz law by more than 200-fold. The Nature Physics finding opens entirely new avenues in quantum sensing and hints at ultracold computing paradigms inspired by the brain.

item.title: AI Gut-Bacteria Test Detects 90% of Colorectal Cancers Without a Colonoscopy
item.description: Scientists built the world's first comprehensive subspecies catalogue of gut bacteria and used it to train an AI model that identified colorectal cancer from simple stool samples with 90% accuracy — nearly matching colonoscopy. A clinical trial with Geneva University Hospitals is now being prepared to bring this non-invasive screen to patients.

item.title: Monarch Butterfly Population Bounces Back 64% in Mexico — Best Since 2018
item.description: Eastern monarchs overwintered across 7.24 acres of Mexican forest this season — a 64% jump from the prior year and the largest area since 2018. Stricter enforcement against illegal logging, community-led habitat care and expanding federal protections are all credited with the most encouraging rebound in years.

item.title: UK Government Backs £1M Plan to Return Golden Eagles to English Skies
item.description: Absent from England for 150 years, the golden eagle may return as early as 2027 after £1 million in government funding was approved to explore reintroduction across eight northern recovery zones. Satellite data shows Scottish birds are already venturing over the border, raising hopes for a swift and natural comeback.

item.title: Chile Signs Decree Protecting 50% of Its Ocean — Third-Largest Marine Reserve on Earth
item.description: President Boric signed landmark protections on March 10 for 386,000 square miles of ocean around the Juan Fernández and Nazca-Desventuradas archipelagos — creating the world's third-largest conserved seascape. The reserve shields whales, turtles and the Juan Fernández fur seal, once feared extinct, and advances the global 30x30 ocean target.

item.title: AI Stool Test Catches 90% of Colorectal Cancers — No Colonoscopy Required
item.description: Researchers mapped gut bacteria at subspecies resolution for the first time and used AI to identify colorectal cancer from stool samples with 90% accuracy, rivalling invasive colonoscopy. In practice, the test could screen millions cheaply and comfortably, reserving colonoscopy only for positive cases. A clinical trial is now being prepared.

item.title: Natural Hormone FGF21 Reverses Obesity by Targeting a Brain Circuit
item.description: University of Oklahoma scientists discovered that the hormone FGF21 reverses obesity in mice by activating a specific hindbrain neuron pathway — boosting metabolism rather than suppressing appetite like GLP-1 drugs. Published in Cell Reports, the finding could lead to more targeted obesity therapies with fewer side effects.

item.title: Starting Hormone Therapy in Perimenopause Cuts Cardiovascular and Cancer Risk
item.description: A 2026 review in Frontiers in Reproductive Health found that women who begin hormone replacement therapy near the onset of menopause — rather than years later — enjoy significantly lower risks of cardiovascular disease and certain cancers. The findings are prompting clinicians to reconsider the optimal timing of a widely used treatment.

item.title: Karol G Makes History as First Latina Artist to Headline Coachella
item.description: Colombian superstar Karol G headlined Coachella 2026 alongside Justin Bieber and Sabrina Carpenter, becoming the first Latina artist ever to top the bill at the iconic California festival. Both sold-out weekends were streamed globally on YouTube, bringing the barrier-breaking performance to millions who couldn't be there in person.

item.title: Thai Wildlife Comeback Transforms 320 Villagers Into Conservation Entrepreneurs
item.description: After the banteng population at Huai Kha Khaeng doubled to 1,400, residents of 19 surrounding villages built a community ecotourism enterprise around wildlife watching, boat tours and cultural experiences. Tourist numbers have grown from dozens to thousands per month, proving that nature recovery and community prosperity can rise together.

item.title: UNESCO Programme Reaches 23.4 Million Young Learners Across Africa
item.description: UNESCO's O3 Programme has secured political commitment to adolescents' health education in nearly 190,000 schools across Eastern, Southern, West and Central Africa — reaching 23.4 million young learners. The initiative equips the next generation with knowledge and agency, making a quiet but profound difference in millions of young lives.

item.title: Michael Jackson Biopic 'Michael' Eyes Record-Breaking $55M+ Opening Weekend
item.description: Directed by Antoine Fuqua and starring Jaafar Jackson as his uncle, the long-awaited biopic 'Michael' opens in US theatres on April 24 and is tracking for the biggest opening in music biopic history — topping 'Bohemian Rhapsody'. Its Berlin premiere drew rave reactions for Jaafar's extraordinary performance in his film debut.

item.title: Coachella 2026 Sells Out Both Weekends as Justin Bieber Returns and Karol G Makes History
item.description: Coachella 2026 sold out in days, with both weekends (April 10–12 and 17–19) drawing fans to the desert for headliners Justin Bieber, Sabrina Carpenter and Karol G. Bieber reportedly earned a record $10 million for his two headline sets, while Karol G became the first Latina artist to top the festival's bill.

item.title: Super Mario Galaxy Movie Opens to Massive Anticipation on April 1
item.description: Nintendo's second animated feature, The Super Mario Galaxy Movie, hit theatres April 1 to enormous fanfare, eyeing one of the biggest animated openings of the decade. Featuring the returning cast and original music by Brian Tyler, the film continues the franchise's Hollywood winning streak and brings the beloved space adventure to life.

item.title: UCLA Shatters WNBA Draft Record With Five First-Round Picks in a Single Draft
item.description: UCLA became the first university in history to land five players in the first round of a WNBA draft, with Lauren Betts, Gabriela Jaquez, Kiki Rice, Angela Dugalic and Gianna Kneepkens all selected on April 14. The historic night came just days after the Bruins won their first-ever NCAA women's basketball championship.

item.title: Ghana's Nations FC Reach Their First-Ever FA Cup Final in Historic Semi-Final Win
item.description: Nations FC defeated Aduana FC 1-0 on April 17 to book a first-ever place in the MTN FA Cup final, where they will face Dreams FC. The Accra club's maiden cup final appearance is a milestone moment for the club, its supporters and the growth of club football across Ghana.

item.title: 2026 Laureus Sport for Good Awards Shine a Light on Sport's Transformative Power
item.description: The 2026 Laureus Sport for Good Award shortlist celebrates organisations harnessing sport to tackle climate change, biodiversity loss and social inequality. From youth programmes in conflict zones to community sports clubs restoring ecosystems, the shortlist is a testament to the extraordinary good that movement and play can inspire.

item.title: Malaysia Becomes Southeast Asia's Fastest-Rising AI and Data Centre Hub
item.description: Capturing 32% of Southeast Asia's AI investment and drawing over US$15 billion in commitments, Malaysia raised its data centre capacity from 120 MW to 690 MW in a single year. Its first solar-powered sovereign AI data centre is now operational, and AI is projected to contribute US$115 billion to Malaysia's GDP by 2030.

item.title: Canada Invests $127 Million in Life Sciences to Fuel Next-Gen Biomanufacturing
item.description: Canada's federal government announced a $127 million investment in Vancouver's Aspect Biosystems and Providence Health Care, supporting tissue bioprinting and life sciences innovation. The funding creates 400 permanent jobs and 268 student co-op positions, cementing Canada's ambition to lead the world in next-generation biomanufacturing.

item.title: Argentina's Bold Reforms Win IMF Upgrade and Renewed Foreign Investor Confidence
item.description: After years of economic turbulence, Argentina's fiscal stabilization plan is delivering results: the IMF upgraded its 2026 outlook for the country in April, citing improved financial conditions, easing inflation, and rising investor confidence. For South America's second-largest economy, the turnaround signals a genuinely hopeful new chapter.

item.title: Canada Launches One of the World's Most Advanced AI Supercomputing Systems
item.description: Canada unveiled an ambitious national AI supercomputing initiative under its Sovereign AI Compute Strategy, giving researchers, universities, and innovators access to world-class computational infrastructure. The programme is designed to keep Canada at the frontier of AI discovery while protecting national data sovereignty and fuelling homegrown innovation.

item.title: Scientists Create Antibodies That Fully Block Epstein-Barr Virus in Landmark Advance
item.description: Researchers at Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center have created powerful antibodies that completely prevent Epstein-Barr virus infection in human immune system models. EBV affects 95% of people worldwide and is linked to several cancers and multiple sclerosis. This breakthrough opens a realistic path to the first protective EBV vaccine, with major benefits for transplant patients.

item.title: Cornell Scientists Achieve Breakthrough Toward Safe, Reversible Male Birth Control
item.description: Cornell researchers developed a compound that completely halts sperm production in mice — reversibly, without hormonal effects — with healthy offspring after treatment ended. Published in PNAS, the study is the most significant advance toward male contraception in decades and lays the scientific groundwork for future human clinical development.

item.title: World Adds Record 692 GW of Renewables in 2025 — The Biggest Clean Energy Year Ever
item.description: IRENA's April 2026 report confirms that global renewable capacity surged by a record 692 GW in 2025, bringing total capacity to 5,149 GW — the largest annual increase ever recorded. Solar led with 511 GW of new additions, and renewables now make up 49.4% of the world's total energy capacity, up from 46.3% in 2024.

item.title: Europe's Wild Bison Bounce Back From Near-Extinction to 9,000 Strong
item.description: A century ago, the European bison was hunted to fewer than 60 individuals in captivity. Today, rewilding programmes across Poland, Romania, Bulgaria, and Spain have helped the population recover to around 9,000. Researchers have also shown that bison herds sequester carbon at remarkable scale — one herd of 170 offsets the emissions of 84,000 petrol cars per year.

item.title: Brazil's Electricity Grid Reaches 84.6% Renewable as Offshore Wind Pipeline Tops 189 GW
item.description: Brazil has crossed a landmark milestone: 84.6% of its electricity capacity is now renewable, with solar growing sixfold in five years and wind and solar jointly supplying nearly 25% of national generation. A 189 GW offshore wind pipeline — backed by a new Offshore Wind Law and World Bank financing — is set to extend Brazil's clean energy lead globally.

item.title: TMS Brain Stimulation Achieves 62% Complete Remission in Severe Depression
item.description: Real-world data from MUSC Health reveals that Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation achieves full remission of depression in 62% of patients — with an 83% response rate overall. Delivered in 20-minute non-invasive sessions over several weeks, TMS reactivates underactive brain regions and is transforming care for patients who haven't responded to antidepressant medication.

item.title: FDA Clears Brain Stimulation Therapy for Teen Depression in Historic Mental Health First
item.description: In a landmark move for adolescent mental health, the FDA has cleared TMS therapy for teenagers with depression — the first time a non-invasive brain stimulation treatment has been approved for young people. The clearance opens a safe, medication-free option for millions of teens struggling with treatment-resistant depression worldwide.

item.title: Scientists Create Antibodies That Fully Block Epstein-Barr Virus — A Major Cancer Cause
item.description: Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center researchers created antibodies that completely prevent EBV infection in lab models. The virus affects 95% of people globally, is linked to several cancers and multiple sclerosis, and is especially dangerous for immunocompromised patients. This advance puts a preventive EBV vaccine — with potentially enormous health benefits — within closer reach.

item.title: Hungary's Historic Democratic Renewal as Magyar Wins With Record 77% Voter Turnout
item.description: In Hungary's most significant election in a generation, Peter Magyar's Tisza party ousted Viktor Orbán with a 53.6% landslide on a record 77% turnout. Tens of thousands lined the Danube as Magyar declared, 'Tonight, truth prevailed over lies,' marking the start of Hungary's democratic renewal and unlocking long-blocked EU aid for Ukraine.

item.title: Bangladesh's PM Named to TIME 100 as Nation Rebuilds Its Democratic Spirit
item.description: Prime Minister Tarique Rahman — who won a sweeping electoral victory in February after 17 years in exile — has joined TIME's 100 Most Influential People of 2026. The recognition celebrates Bangladesh's democratic renewal and the country's determination to build a more open, transparent, and prosperous future for its 170 million citizens.

item.title: Australia Celebrates Indigenous Scientists Restoring Native Seeds and Food Systems
item.description: The Australian Academy of Science's 2026 Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Scientist Award honours Dr Cassandra Sedran-Price and Mr Jacob Birch for research rooted in traditional knowledge. Their work — establishing community seed banks and reviving drought-tolerant native grains — weaves scientific rigour with cultural wisdom to build climate-resilient food futures.

item.title: V&A East Museum Opens With Landmark Exhibition on 125 Years of Black British Music
item.description: London's brand-new V&A East Museum opens on 18 April 2026 with 'The Music Is Black: A British Story' — a landmark free museum tracing 125 years of Black British musical culture through 200+ objects, fashion, film, and sound. Running until January 2027, the show is already heralded as one of the most significant cultural openings of the decade.

item.title: Euphoria Season 3 Premieres to Record 8.5 Million Viewers, Up 44% From Season 2
item.description: After a four-year wait, Euphoria returned on 12 April 2026 to its biggest audience ever — 8.5 million US viewers in just three days, a 44% jump over Season 2's debut. The show trended globally on social media with #Euphoria at No. 1 on X for six consecutive hours, cementing its place as the defining drama of its generation.

item.title: New York's Grand Duchamp Retrospective Invites the World to Reimagine Art's Possibilities
item.description: A sweeping Marcel Duchamp retrospective has opened in New York City, drawing visitors from around the world to reconsider the boundaries of creativity and expression. The exhibition spans his entire career — from early paintings to the readymades that changed art history forever — offering a joyful, mind-bending celebration of artistic freedom and curiosity.

item.title: Rory McIlroy Defends Masters Title, Joins Tiger, Faldo, and Nicklaus in Elite History
item.description: Rory McIlroy claimed his second consecutive Masters title on April 12, 2026, becoming only the fourth player ever to successfully defend at Augusta — joining Nick Faldo, Tiger Woods, and Jack Nicklaus. Opening with record-breaking rounds of 67-65, the Northern Irishman held off Scottie Scheffler to claim his sixth major overall.

item.title: Venezuela Wins World Baseball Classic for the First Time in History
item.description: In a heart-stopping final at Miami's loanDepot Park, Venezuela edged the United States 3-2 to capture the World Baseball Classic trophy for the very first time. Eugenio Suárez's ninth-inning clutch double settled the contest, igniting nationwide celebrations and making Venezuela the first South American country to win the sport's premier international prize.

item.title: Corbin Carroll Makes MLB History in His 500th Game, Joining DiMaggio and Gehrig
item.description: Arizona Diamondbacks outfielder Corbin Carroll celebrated his 500th MLB game on April 8, 2026, with a 3-for-5 performance featuring two doubles and a triple. With 84 home runs, 124 stolen bases, and 46 triples across 500 games, Carroll became the only player in MLB history to achieve those numbers — joining an all-time legends list alongside DiMaggio and Gehrig.
